transcript:
[doctor] well good morning emily how are you
[patient] good morning
[doctor] how are you doing
[patient] doing okay i'm okay
[doctor] i understand you're having some sudden visual distortions and some blurring of your vision is that right
[patient] it is yes
[doctor] okay so what's going on
[patient] sorry to put you off i do n't think i have that script
[doctor] oh
[patient] maybe a different one i wonder if patient has history of
[doctor] macular degeneration yeah yeah i was because we do n't we do n't to do the intro anymore i was just going with that you you're coming in with sudden visual distortion and blurring of vision so i'll just you know i'll just say i'll just go ahead and go into this technician part so it looks like the technician noticed that you've been having a decrease in vision when reading and straight lines and your door frames have been looking crooked to you is that right
[patient] it is yes
[doctor] okay what kind of venture are we gon na go into do you do you have the the script or no it it just talks
[patient] but it's it's not presenting like like how you're reading it like how you're talking about it is it kinda what i'm saying because like after that i'm supposed to say yeah whenever i take my husband out to like a nice restaurant i have trouble adjusting to those dim lights but i'm not really getting the question of like so what's causing it or where do you have this happening stuff like that so i'm a little confused
[doctor] okay i mean i can just straight out follow the script just so we can kinda get it rolling so so i said so hi so it looks like the technician noticed that you've been having a decrease in vision when reading and straight lines in your door frames have been looking crooked to you
[patient] yeah and whenever i take my husband out to a nice restaurant i have trouble adjusting to those dim lights
[doctor] uh okay so do you have a favorite restaurant you like to go to
[patient] i do mcdonalds is one of my favorite here in arlington
[doctor] that sounds good what what do you what's your favorite there
[patient] they have a great egg plant parmajon one of my favorite so i just go ahead and go with that
[doctor] that sounds really nice very good
[patient] it's just
[doctor] yeah okay well so it looks like you're still smoking a pack of cigarettes a day
[patient] yes i tried to stop smoking every now and then but it's still been hard to totally stop
[doctor] okay and how is your blood pressure been doing are you keeping up with your medication
[patient] i take my blood pressure when i can remember to do so
[doctor] okay yeah it's really important to keep up with your blood pressure check that regularly i'm totally going off script and you know might want to keep a log of that as well and check your blood pressures regularly so okay well let me do a just a quick exam of your eyes if you can take off your glasses and just rest your chin on the chin rest okay so the anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes right eye the cornea is clear no mvi pciol cup to disc is . two fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached three hundred and sixty degrees without any tears or holes rpe changes in the fovea posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula thickening of the retina presence of subretinal fluid and exudates accumulation positive for hemorrhage left eye the cornea is clear no mvi cup to disc is . three rpe changes in the fovea no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid retina is attached okay so i reviewed the results of your diagnostic tests so for the oct so it shows drusen deposit and the retinal pigment epithelium subretinal fluid accumulation choroidal neovascular membrane can be visualized fluorescein angiography reveals focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neurovascular age related macular degeneration so the diagnostic tests and the eye exam are really indicative of neovascular age related macular degeneration so this is advanced to a stage that usually leads to more severe vision loss and it really happens when those abnormal blood vessels start to grow beneath the retina and they will leak fluid and blood and hints the name wet amd and can create a large blind spot in the center of the visual field and this is really with the most common cause for severe loss of vision so my plan is to give you an injection of lucentes once a month it's injected into the vitreous portion of the eye after it's been numbed and injections are given regularly over a period of time it may slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss but however the disease and loss it may also progress despite treatment do you have any questions
[patient] i do not no that sounds great
[doctor] okay
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Changes in vision.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient smokes approximately 1 pack of cigarettes per day.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Eyes: Reports visual distortions and blurred vision.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Eyes
- Examination: Anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes.
Right eye: The cornea is clear. No MVI. PCIOL cup to disc is 0.2. Fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees without any tears or holes. RPE changes in the fovea. Posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid and exudates accumulation. Positive for hemorrhage.
Left eye: The cornea is clear. No MVI. Cup to disc is 0.3. RPE changes in the fovea. No hemorrhage or subretinal fluid. Retina is attached.

RESULTS

Optical coherence tomography shows drusen deposit. Retinal pigment epithelium with subretinal fluid accumulation. The choroidal neovascular membrane can be visualized. Fluorescein angiography reveals focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
- Medical Reasoning: Based on her diagnostic testing and eye exam, this is at an advanced stage.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed the nature of the condition, as well as the recommendation for Lucentis injections. I explained the procedure in detail and advised the patient of the potential outcomes.
- Medical Treatment: We will proceed with a once-monthly Lucenti's injections.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Emily, presented with sudden visual distortions and blurring of her vision. She reports difficulty adjusting to dim lighting, specifically when dining at restaurants. She denies any other symptoms.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of macular degeneration. She is a smoker, consuming one pack of cigarettes per day, and has been trying to quit but has not been successful. She reports taking her blood pressure medication sporadically, but does not keep a regular log of her readings.

Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms outside of her visual disturbances.

Current medications: I prescribed blood pressure medication, but the specific medication and dosing information was not provided.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The anterior segment examination revealed posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes, clear corneas, and no MVI. The cup-to-disc ratio is 0.2 in the right eye and 0.3 in the left eye. The fundus examination of the right eye showed the retina to be attached, with no tears or holes. The posterior segment exam revealed drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, subretinal fluid, and exudates accumulation, and positive for hemorrhage in the left eye.

Test Results: The diagnostic tests, including OCT and fluorescein angiography, revealed drusen deposit, subretinal fluid accumulation, choroidal neovascular membrane, and focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has neovascular age-related macular degeneration, a condition that typically leads to severe vision loss due to the growth of abnormal blood vessels beneath the retina, causing fluid and blood leakage.

Problem list: Neovascular age-related macular degeneration; active.

Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis, an injection to be administered once a month into the vitreous portion of the eye, to slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss. Injections will be given regularly over a period of time.

Follow-up: The patient will return for follow-up appointments as needed to monitor the progression of the disease and adjust treatment as necessary.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with sudden visual distortions and blurring of vision. She reports that straight lines and door frames appear crooked to her, and she has trouble adjusting to dim lights, particularly in restaurants. She mentions that she smokes a pack of cigarettes a day and has difficulty stopping. Her blood pressure is not well-controlled, as she only takes her medication when she remembers.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of macular degeneration, which is relevant to her current presentation. She does not mention any other past medical conditions, social history, or travel history.

Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs that are relevant to her initial presentation.

Current medications: The patient is not specified to be taking any medications other than her blood pressure medication, which she takes sporadically.

Objective:

The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees without tears or holes, RPE changes in the fovea. The posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, thickening of the retina, presence of subretinal fluid, and exudates accumulation positive for hemorrhage in the left eye.

Test Results: The OCT shows drusen deposit, retinal pigment epithelium subretinal fluid accumulation, choroidal neovascular membrane, and fluorescein angiography reveals focal areas of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has advanced neovascular age-related macular degeneration.

Problem list: Neovascular age-related macular degeneration; active; Macular degeneration; past; Hypertension; unknown; Smoking; unknown.

Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis once a month, which will be injected into the vitreous portion of the eye after it has been numbed. The injections will be given regularly over a period of time to slow the rate of vision decline or stop further vision loss. However, the disease may also progress despite treatment.

Follow-up: I plan to see the patient again to monitor the effectiveness of the treatment and to address any further questions or concerns she may have.
